title: Painting over bare plaster: the mist coat
subtitle: Apply a diluted first coat to seal new plaster before the finish coats
excerpt: Bare plaster absorbs paint at a rate that standard emulsion cannot compensate for. A mist coat (emulsion diluted with water) is applied first to seal the surface, equalise suction, and give the finish coats something stable to bond to.
sourceNotes: City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A diluted first coat of emulsion paint applied to bare or newly plastered surfaces. Emulsion diluted 10 to 20 per cent with water penetrates the plaster surface and seals it, equalising absorption before the undiluted finish coats are applied.

The tendency of bare or porous plaster to absorb moisture rapidly from applied coatings. High suction plaster pulls water out of paint too fast, leaving a thin, uneven film that does not bond properly to the surface.

content.text: New plaster has high
suction

. Paint brushed onto a freshly cured surface is absorbed within seconds, leaving a thin, patchy layer with no film strength. Even two full-strength coats will show uneven sheen and colour variation. A
mist coat
costs almost nothing extra in time and prevents the need for a third or fourth coat.
When to use a mist coat
Any freshly plastered wall or ceiling that has just cured.
A plaster patch on an otherwise painted wall where the patch shows lighter than the surrounding area.
After stripping the old plaster to bare render and replastering a room.
When not to use a mist coat
Do not apply a mist coat to wet or damp plaster. Wait until the plaster is a uniform colour, completely dry to the touch, and not cold.
Do not use a mist coat on previously painted walls. It will simply thin the first coat without providing any benefit.
How to apply the mist coat
Step 1, mix the mist coat.

Pour the emulsion into a clean bucket and dilute with water. The correct ratio for a standard gypsum-finish plaster is 1 part water to 9 parts emulsion (approximately 10 per cent). On a very porous or old-lime surface, dilute to 20 per cent water. The mist coat should be thin enough to penetrate the surface but not so thin that it runs. Stir thoroughly.
Step 2, apply by brush or roller.

Roll the mist coat onto the wall in the same bands as a standard coat. Cut in with the brush at perimeter edges. Do not be alarmed by the uneven appearance: the diluted paint will be absorbed differently by the plaster in different areas, giving a patchy look. This is correct. The mist coat is not a cosmetic coat; it is a surface treatment.
Step 3, allow to dry fully.

A mist coat dries faster than a full-strength coat because it is so thin. Allow at least 4 hours in a ventilated room at 18 degrees. The surface is ready for the finish coat when it feels uniformly dry to the touch and shows no cold or damp patches.
Step 4, apply full-strength finish coats.

Apply two full-strength coats of emulsion. After the mist coat, the surface accepts paint evenly and the finish coat lies at a consistent film thickness. The two-coat finish coats over a mist coat will typically give the same coverage as three coats applied without the mist preparation.
What it looks like when it is right

After the two finish coats have dried, the surface should show a uniform sheen and colour across both the patched and original plaster areas. Hold a lamp at a low angle to check for patches or uneven sheen. If patches remain, apply a third full-strength coat.
Common mistakes
Applying the mist coat to damp plaster.
Plaster must be fully cured before any paint is applied. New skim coat: allow at least 1 week in a heated room. Thicker coats or old lime plaster: allow up to 4 weeks.
Using a proprietary 'plaster primer' instead of diluted emulsion.
Some trade primers are designed for this job; they work. But diluted matt emulsion is the standard domestic approach, costs nothing extra, and uses paint already on site.
